extraction_scope: All manifesto sections, subsections, section intro/meta text, and numbered promises/points from the electoral promises portion.

points.text: The Party will take all necessary measures to make the recommendations of the committee, led by retired Supreme Court Justice Kurian Joseph, a subject of nationwide debate. By collaborating with like-minded movements, the party will work to halt the authoritarian tendencies of the Union Government and firmly re-establish the rights of the states.
points.meta_information_text: The principle of State Autonomy is the enduring legacy that Arignar Anna authored as an immortal charter! Autonomy for the states and federalism at the Centre” was one of the five core slogans that Kalaignar championed throughout his life. This principle is fundamental to strengthening India. Since a constitutional amendment is essential to firmly establish and implement this vision, the Dravidian Model government has appointed a committee headed by retired Supreme Court Judge, Justice Kurian Joseph to explore the necessary legal pathways. The committee has submitted the first volume of its report to the Government of Tamil Nadu. It highlights the excessive concentration of power within the Union Government and the rapid erosion of state rights. The report also lists the necessary steps to rectify this politically unhealthy trend. Furthermore, the committee emphasises a structural reset comparable in ambition to the economic reforms of 1991. The report underscores that the historical context in which the Indian Constitution was framed is vastly different from the realities of today. It warns that in a country like India, home to diverse groups, uniformity is extremely dangerous. Instead, the committee’s report strongly recommends that a strong federal structure is the need of the hour. As a continuation of this vision, in the Dravidian Model 2.0 governance…

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu panel headed by Justice Kurian Joseph calls for structural reset
evidence.snippet: Part I of the report was tabled in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly in February 2026, proposing wide-ranging constitutional amendments.

plan_existence.notes: The committee was established by the DMK government in April 2025. The manifesto promise focuses on propagating its findings and building a national consensus, effectively continuing the work already initiated by the state government.

legal.comments: While the state can form committees and debate reports, the actual implementation of recommendations requires major Constitutional amendments (Articles 356, 368, etc.) by the Union Parliament.

administrative.comments: The state has already demonstrated administrative capacity by forming the committee and tabling the report; organizing debates is well within state machinery limits.

timeline.comments: Nationwide debate and collaboration with other states can be achieved within a 5-year term, though legislative success depends on the Union government.

political.comments: This aligns with the DMK's core ideology of state autonomy and is likely to find support among other regional parties in India.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is highly feasible as a political and advocacy goal. Since the committee already exists and has submitted reports, the next step of 'nationwide debate' is a logical progression of current state policy.
key_risks: Lack of cooperation from the Union Government
Difficulty in achieving consensus among diverse regional parties
Constitutional hurdles in implementing structural resets

evidence.title: The Politics of Language in India
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu's ruling party, the DMK, has historically built its politics around linguistic pride and anti-Hindi sentiments, following a two-language policy.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu has officially followed a two-language policy (Tamil and English) since 1968. The promise is a reaffirmation of this long-standing state policy in opposition to perceived Union government pressures.

legal.comments: The state has the legal authority to determine its own official language policy under Article 345 of the Constitution.

administrative.comments: The administrative framework for the two-language policy is already fully operational in Tamil Nadu's schools and government offices.

timeline.comments: This is a continuation of an existing status quo; no new timeline is required.

political.comments: This is a cornerstone of Dravidian politics with near-unanimous public support in Tamil Nadu.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible continuation of existing state policy. It serves as a political stance against the Union's language initiatives rather than a new administrative program.
key_risks: Friction with Union government over National Education Policy (NEP) implementation
Potential loss of central funding for schemes tied to three-language formula compliance

evidence.title: DMK will work to get 'official' tag for all Eighth Schedule languages
evidence.snippet: CM Stalin stated the DMK government will work to make all 22 languages in the Eighth Schedule the official languages of the Union government.

plan_existence.notes: Currently, Article 343 defines Hindi as the official language of the Union, with English as an associate official language. No provision exists to make all 22 languages official for Union administration.

fiscal.comments: Making 22 languages official for all Union communications would involve astronomical costs for translation, simultaneous interpretation, and multi-lingual documentation across all central ministries.

legal.comments: Requires a major amendment to Article 343 of the Constitution, which needs a two-thirds majority in both houses of Parliament and ratification by half the states.

administrative.comments: The administrative complexity of conducting all Union business in 22 languages is currently beyond the capacity of the central secretariat.

timeline.comments: Unlikely to be achieved in a 5-year term given the requirement for a national constitutional consensus.

political.comments: While popular in non-Hindi states, it faces significant opposition from the Hindi-speaking belt and the current Union leadership.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a symbolic political demand with extremely low feasibility. The legal and administrative hurdles at the Union level make it nearly impossible to implement in the near future.
key_risks: Constitutional deadlock
Extreme administrative inefficiency
Prohibitive fiscal costs for translation and infrastructure

evidence.title: What stops Tamil being made the official language of the Madras High Court?
evidence.snippet: The Union government has received proposals from Tamil Nadu to permit the use of Tamil in High Court proceedings, but the Supreme Court has repeatedly rejected such proposals.

plan_existence.notes: While Article 348(2) allows for the use of state languages in High Courts with Presidential consent, the Union government and the Supreme Court have consistently declined Tamil Nadu's requests, unlike in Rajasthan, MP, UP, and Bihar where Hindi is permitted.

fiscal.comments: Minimal fiscal impact, primarily involving costs for translation services and updated legal databases in Tamil.

legal.comments: Requires the consent of the President of India under Article 348(2). Past attempts have been blocked by the Supreme Court's 'Full Court' decisions which the Union government follows.

administrative.comments: Requires judges to be proficient in Tamil, which complicates the current practice of transferring judges across different state High Courts.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented quickly if Presidential consent is granted, but obtaining that consent is a long-standing hurdle.

political.comments: Strong political consensus in Tamil Nadu across all major parties (DMK, AIADMK) and the legal fraternity.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is legally possible but practically stalled by the Union executive and the higher judiciary. It remains a persistent demand rather than an immediately implementable plan.
key_risks: Continued refusal of consent by the President/Union Government
Opposition from the Supreme Court of India
Logistical challenges for non-Tamil speaking judges

evidence.title: T.N. for including contribution to country's economy as a criterion for devolution: Panagariya
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu's recommendation to the 16th Finance Commission was to go back to using the 1971 Census population figures for tax devolution.

evidence.title: Which states gain most: 16th Finance Commission tax devolution formula explained
evidence.snippet: The 15th Finance Commission used 2011 census data, which penalized states like Tamil Nadu that implemented population control; the 16th FC report was published in February 2026.

plan_existence.notes: The Finance Commission currently uses 2011 Census data as per its terms of reference. Tamil Nadu has consistently advocated for the 1971 Census to protect its share of tax devolution, which has declined as its population growth slowed relative to other states.

legal.comments: The Terms of Reference (ToR) for the Finance Commission are set by the President of India (Union Cabinet). A state government has no legal power to dictate these terms, only to recommend them.

administrative.comments: Implementing a different census year in a mathematical formula for tax devolution is administratively simple once the policy is decided.

timeline.comments: The 16th Finance Commission report is already active as of early 2026; changes would likely apply to the next commission or require a mid-term amendment to the ToR.

political.comments: Highly contentious at the national level as it would reduce the share of high-population northern states, leading to significant political opposition in Parliament.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a policy advocacy point rather than an implementable state scheme. While it aligns with Tamil Nadu's long-term fiscal interests, the power to execute this lies entirely with the Union Government.
key_risks: Rejection by the Union Government
Political backlash from high-population states
Legal challenges regarding the use of outdated demographic data

plan_existence.notes: The 'One Nation, One Election' proposal is a central government initiative currently in the legislative process (JPC review). The manifesto promise is a demand for the withdrawal of this central legislation.

legal.comments: A state government cannot withdraw a bill introduced in the Union Parliament. It can only pass a resolution in the State Assembly expressing opposition.

administrative.comments: The state can refuse to cooperate with certain administrative aspects, but the primary legislative control is central.

political.comments: Depends entirely on the balance of power in the Union Parliament and the ability of regional parties to form a blocking coalition.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Govt. seeks to transfer of education back to 'State List'
evidence.snippet: Education was moved from the State List to the Concurrent List by the 42nd Amendment Act of 1976.

evidence.title: DMK wants education under State List
evidence.snippet: DMK MP P Wilson moved a Private Member Bill in 2022 to amend the Seventh Schedule and bring education back to the State List.

plan_existence.notes: Education is currently Entry 25 in the Concurrent List (List III) of the Seventh Schedule. Moving it back to the State List (List II) would require a constitutional amendment.

legal.comments: Requires a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368, necessitating a two-thirds majority in both Houses of Parliament and ratification by at least half of the State Legislatures. A single state cannot achieve this.

administrative.comments: If achieved, the state would have full autonomy over curriculum and admissions, but would lose central funding and standardized national frameworks.

timeline.comments: Extremely unlikely to be achieved within a 5-year term given the current national political climate and the complexity of constitutional amendments.

political.comments: No national consensus exists for this move; most central governments prefer education to remain in the Concurrent List to implement national policies like the NEP.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a fundamental federalist demand that is legally and politically nearly impossible for a state government to fulfill on its own. It serves as a long-term ideological goal rather than a feasible election promise.
key_risks: Failure to secure two-thirds majority in Parliament
Lack of support from other states
Potential loss of central education grants

evidence.snippet: The legislation passed in 2022 was returned by the President in late 2025, effectively blocking the state's attempt to take over VC appointments from the Governor.

plan_existence.notes: The Tamil Nadu government already passed legislation in 2022 to take over VC appointments. However, the President of India withheld assent in December 2025, returning the bills to the state.

legal.comments: The President has already withheld assent for this specific proposal. Re-passing the same bill may lead to a similar outcome unless there is a change in the Union Government's stance or a successful legal challenge in the Supreme Court.

timeline.comments: Depends on a lengthy legal battle or a change in the central executive's advice to the President.

political.comments: Directly pits the state against the Governor and the Union Government, leading to institutional friction.

feasibility.overall_comments: The state has already attempted this and failed due to the withholding of Presidential assent. Fulfilling this promise requires overcoming a significant constitutional hurdle that has already blocked the previous attempt.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu moves Supreme Court against President's refusal to grant assent to anti-NEET Bill
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government moved the Supreme Court in November 2025 challenging the President's decision to withhold assent to the state's NEET exemption bill.

plan_existence.notes: While the state has passed a bill to exempt itself from NEET, it has not been implemented due to the withholding of Presidential assent. The promise represents a continuation of the state's legal and political struggle against centralized entrance exams.

legal.comments: Education is in the Concurrent List, but the National Medical Commission Act mandates NEET. Overriding a Central law requires Presidential assent under Article 254(2), which has been consistently denied and is now under litigation.

administrative.comments: Tamil Nadu has decades of experience conducting medical admissions based on Class 12 marks; the administrative machinery for the previous system remains viable.

timeline.comments: The timeline is entirely dependent on a Supreme Court verdict or a change in Union government policy, neither of which is guaranteed within a 5-year term.

political.comments: The move has high domestic popularity in Tamil Nadu as it is framed as a matter of state autonomy and social justice for rural students.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is popular but faces a near-insurmountable legal wall. It is more of a political stance on federalism than a policy that the state can implement unilaterally.
key_risks: Supreme Court rejection of the state's plea
Constitutional deadlock with the Union government
Potential non-recognition of degrees by national medical bodies

evidence.title: GST council compromises autonomy of states: TN
evidence.snippet: Tamil Nadu has consistently argued that the GST Council's voting weightage gives the Union Government an effective veto in fiscal matters.

plan_existence.notes: The GST Council is a constitutional body (Article 279A). The 'unilateralism' refers to the voting structure where the Union has 1/3rd weightage, effectively allowing it to veto any decision. No mechanism currently exists to 'curb' this without constitutional change.

legal.comments: Curbing the Union's power in the GST Council requires an amendment to Article 279A of the Constitution, which requires a two-thirds majority in Parliament and ratification by half the states.

administrative.comments: Administrative changes to voting procedures are simple, but the political consensus required to initiate them is absent.

timeline.comments: Constitutional amendments of this nature rarely occur within a single state's legislative term without overwhelming national support.

political.comments: While other opposition-ruled states might support this, the Union government is unlikely to voluntarily relinquish its veto power.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a structural demand for federal reform that cannot be fulfilled by a state government alone. It serves as a rhetorical commitment to state rights.
key_risks: Lack of national political consensus
Rigid constitutional amendment process
Opposition from the Union government

evidence.title: States to remain 'net gainers' of GST rate rationalisation: SBI Research
evidence.snippet: Currently, GST is shared 50:50 between the Centre and states, though states effectively receive nearly 70% after devolution of the Centre's share.

plan_existence.notes: The statutory split of GST is currently 50% Central GST (CGST) and 50% State GST (SGST). The promise seeks to increase the statutory SGST portion to 70%.

fiscal.comments: Increasing the state share to 70% would result in a massive revenue loss for the Union government, potentially destabilizing the national budget and central welfare schemes.

legal.comments: Requires amendments to the CGST and SGST Acts. Such amendments must be recommended by the GST Council, where the Union government holds a veto.

administrative.comments: The GSTN infrastructure is already designed to handle percentage-based splits; changing the ratio is administratively trivial once the law is passed.

timeline.comments: Negotiations in the GST Council and Finance Commission are protracted; a 20% shift is unlikely to be settled within five years.

political.comments: Many states would support this demand, but the Union government will resist any move that significantly reduces its divisible pool of taxes.

feasibility.overall_comments: A high-stakes fiscal demand that challenges the current center-state financial balance. While it aligns with state interests, its realization is highly improbable without a major national policy shift.
key_risks: Union government veto in the GST Council
National fiscal deficit concerns
Inter-state disagreement on devolution formulas

evidence.title: Private Member's Bill on Judicial Diversity - Drishti IAS
evidence.snippet: The Indian Constitution does not mandate reservations for SC, ST, OBC, or women in the higher judiciary (under Articles 124 and 217). Diversity relies entirely on the discretion of the Collegium.

evidence.title: Constitution (Amendment) Bill, 2026 Proposes Judicial Diversity - SCC Online
evidence.snippet: DMK Rajya Sabha MP P. Wilson introduced the Constitution (Amendment) Bill, 2026, proposing constitutional reforms aimed at judicial diversity and reservation in the higher judiciary.

plan_existence.notes: Currently, there is no constitutional provision for reservation in the appointment of High Court or Supreme Court judges. Appointments are governed by the Collegium system. While a Private Member's Bill has been introduced by a TN representative, it has not been enacted into law.

legal.comments: Requires a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368, necessitating a two-thirds majority in both Houses of Parliament and ratification by half of the State Legislatures.

administrative.comments: Implementation would require restructuring the Memorandum of Procedure (MoP) for judicial appointments, which involves complex coordination between the Executive and the Judiciary.

timeline.comments: Passing a Constitutional Amendment is a multi-year process and highly dependent on the political will of the Union Government, making it unlikely within a single state term without central alignment.

political.comments: Strongly aligns with the 'Dravidian Model' and social justice politics in Tamil Nadu, likely to receive high local support but significant opposition at the national level.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a policy advocacy stance rather than a direct state-level implementation. Its success depends entirely on federal legislative dynamics and constitutional hurdles.
key_risks: Lack of consensus in the Union Parliament
Potential judicial strike-down if deemed to violate the 'Basic Structure' (judicial independence)
Resistance from the Supreme Court Collegium

evidence.title: Supreme Court Rejects Tamil Nadu's Plea On 50% OBC Quota In AIQ
evidence.snippet: The Supreme Court dismissed petitions by the Tamil Nadu government seeking 50% OBC reservation in AIQ seats, maintaining the central pool's distinct status.

plan_existence.notes: The All India Quota (15% UG, 50% PG) was created by the Supreme Court to ensure merit-based access across states. Abolishing it requires a change in central policy or a judicial reversal, which the state government has been advocating for years without success.

fiscal.comments: No direct fiscal cost to the state; it is a policy change regarding seat allocation.

legal.comments: AIQ is a Supreme Court-mandated system. The state cannot unilaterally abolish it as it falls under the jurisdiction of the Union Government and the Judiciary (Entry 66 of Union List).

administrative.comments: Implementation would require a complete overhaul of the national medical counseling process (MCC), which the state does not control.

timeline.comments: Unlikely to be achieved in 5 years given the long-standing legal precedents and central government stance.

political.comments: High political resonance in Tamil Nadu as it aligns with the state's 'social justice' and 'state autonomy' narratives.

feasibility.overall_comments: While politically popular, the promise is legally and administratively infeasible at the state level as it requires the Union Government to dismantle a system protected by the Supreme Court.
key_risks: Judicial strike-down
Conflict with Union Government
Potential loss of seats for TN students in other states if AIQ is abolished reciprocally

evidence.title: Union Cabinet 'approves' caste enumeration in 2027 Census

plan_existence.notes: The promise depends on the Union Government's conduct of the National Census. While the state has urged the Union, it cannot conduct the 'National Population Census' itself, though it could conduct a state-level 'Caste Survey' (like Bihar).

timeline.comments: Depends on the Union's timeline for the delayed 2021 census, now expected around 2026-2027.

political.comments: Strongly supported by almost all regional parties in Tamil Nadu to justify the 69% reservation quota.

feasibility.overall_comments: The feasibility is moderate because the state is dependent on the Union Government for the 'National Census' component. However, the state can use the data for 'appropriate representation' once it is available.

Data accuracy and enumeration complexities

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu's initiative to translate medical and academic textbooks
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu Text Book and Educational Services Corporation (TNTBESC) translates medical and academic textbooks to bridge language barriers for students, having published 875 books so far.

evidence.title: TN govt to create corpus of Tamil words for technical terms
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government would create a corpus of Tamil words for terms to be used in various sectors like medical, engineering, and technology.

plan_existence.notes: While the TNTB&ESC currently translates major textbooks (like Gray's Anatomy) and creates technical terminologies, the promise to translate international research journals 'immediately' represents a significant expansion in scale and speed compared to the current book-focused model.

fiscal.comments: Translating international journals 'immediately' is extremely expensive. Current book translation projects have budgets around ₹10 crore for 500 books; journals produce thousands of articles monthly, requiring a massive recurring outlay for copyright and expert translation.

legal.comments: Requires complex international copyright negotiations with journal publishers (e.g., Elsevier, Springer) for translation rights, which can be legally cumbersome and costly.

administrative.comments: The 'immediate' requirement is administratively daunting. It requires a vast network of subject-matter experts who are also proficient in technical Tamil, a resource that is currently scarce.

timeline.comments: A pilot program for specific high-impact journals could be launched within 5 years, but a comprehensive 'immediate' translation system for all genres is unlikely to be fully operational in one term.

political.comments: Strongly aligns with the linguistic pride and 'Tamil Medium' promotion policies of major TN parties; likely to receive high public and academic support.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is a logical but highly ambitious expansion of existing textbook translation efforts. The primary hurdles are the sheer volume of global research and the high cost of acquiring translation rights and expert labor.
key_risks: High recurring fiscal burden
Copyright/IP legal hurdles
Shortage of qualified technical translators
Sustainability of 'immediate' delivery

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Language Lab Vision
evidence.snippet: The School Education Department of Tamil Nadu is establishing language labs in government schools for grades 6 to 8 to provide students exposure to English and Tamil through interactive digital tools.

evidence.title: TN bats for English, plans language labs
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu school department announced a plan to set up English language labs in at least 6,000 government schools at a budget of ₹30 crore.

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu already has a massive 'Hi-Tech Lab' infrastructure in over 6,000 schools. While recent focus (Mozhigal project) has been on English proficiency, the infrastructure is designed for bilingual (English/Tamil) use. This promise emphasizes a dedicated or enhanced Tamil-specific module.

fiscal.comments: The state has already invested over ₹500 crore in Hi-Tech Lab infrastructure. Adding Tamil-specific software and content is a relatively low marginal cost compared to the initial hardware setup.

legal.comments: No legal or constitutional barriers; falls entirely within the state's jurisdiction over school education.

administrative.comments: The delivery mechanism (Hi-Tech Labs) is already in place. The challenge lies in developing high-quality interactive Tamil content and training teachers to use it effectively.

timeline.comments: Can be easily rolled out across existing labs within 1-2 years as it primarily involves software deployment and teacher orientation.

political.comments: Promoting Tamil language through technology is a core political objective in the state and faces no opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible promise because it leverages existing multi-crore hardware infrastructure. It essentially calls for a content update and a shift in focus toward Tamil within the already established digital lab ecosystem.
key_risks: Underutilization of existing labs
Quality of digital Tamil content
Need for consistent teacher training
